strategic_overview: This problem is best viewed as a PL/chord-foliation version of the optimal embedded paper Möbius band theorem. The quotient Sigma/<G_beta> is a compact Möbius band, and the clean triangulation gives a finite boundary-vertex triangulation after quotienting. The decisive observation is that the squeeze inequalities point in exactly the right direction for Schwartz's T-pattern proof: boundary arcs become shorter in the image, while every apex-to-ridge bend in a clean triangle should become longer. Thus the lower bound should be proved by adapting the bend/T-pattern plus cut-open trapezoid argument from The Optimal Paper Moebius Band, https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.12641, especially Lemma 1.3 (T), Lemma 1.4 (G), Lemma 2.3, and the abstract oriented-line Lemma 3.1, rather than by using Sadowsky/Wunderlich variational machinery.

directions.name: Direct PL T-pattern lower bound
directions.description: Pass to the compact quotient M_beta=Sigma/<G_beta>. For each clean triangle, call its boundary edge the ridge and the opposite boundary vertex the apex. The family of all apex-to-ridge segments should form a circle of bends after gluing adjacent triangles along their non-boundary edges. Prove that a squeeze map lengthens every such bend, not just the two sides: if q(s)=|F(a)-F((1-s)r0+s r1)|^2-|a-((1-s)r0+s r1)|^2, then q(0),q(1)>0 and q''=2(|F(r1)-F(r0)|^2-|r1-r0|^2)<0, so q>0 on the ridge. Then orient the image supporting lines by lifting to Sigma and orienting from y=0 to y=1; one circuit reverses orientation because G_beta flips y. Apply the oriented-line Borsuk-Ulam argument to get perpendicular intersecting supporting lines. Cut along one bend T with horizontal displacement t and reuse the trapezoid proof: beta=ell(H)+t=ell(D)-t, ell(H)>|T'|>sqrt(1+t^2), and the T-pattern triangle gives ell(D)>sqrt(5+t^2). Hence beta>max(sqrt(1+t^2)+t, sqrt(5+t^2)-t)>=sqrt(3).
key_ideas: The numerical sqrt(3) should come entirely from the same one-variable minimax as Schwartz: h(t)=sqrt(1+t^2)+t and d(t)=sqrt(5+t^2)-t balance at t=1/sqrt(3).
The squeeze assumptions are stronger than isometry for the needed inequalities: boundary length in the domain dominates image boundary length, while bend image length dominates flat bend length.
The clean-triangulation combinatorics should replace smooth developable rulings: triangles give intervals of bends, and their side-edge identifications give a bend circle.
relevant_techniques: The Optimal Paper Moebius Band, https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.12641, Lemma 1.3 (T): Borsuk-Ulam forcing of an embedded T-pattern from a cyclic bend family.
The Optimal Paper Moebius Band, https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.12641, Lemma 3.1: abstract oriented-line path lemma; this is the cleanest topological input for the PL bend-line family.
The Optimal Paper Moebius Band, https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.12641, Lemma 1.4 (G) and Lemma 2.3: cut-open trapezoid plus triangle reflection estimate yielding the sqrt(3) bound.
On Nearly Optimal Paper Moebius Bands, https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.00572, recalled Lemma G: the h(t), d(t) formulation is the most compact version of the metric calculation.
directions.potential_obstacles: The main issue is proving that the Borsuk-Ulam zero gives a nondegenerate T-pattern usable in the cut argument. In a PL clean triangulation, many bends in one triangle share the same apex, so the oriented-line lemma could return two perpendicular lines meeting at a common boundary vertex rather than two disjoint bends. This is a technical, not numerical, obstruction.

directions.name: Regularize bend fans to remove degeneracies
directions.description: If the direct bend-circle argument produces degenerate T-patterns inside a single apex fan, introduce a small epsilon-regularization of the bend family. Split boundary vertices or truncate fan neighborhoods so that leaves have disjoint endpoints and form a genuine continuous foliation by cross-segments, while changing all length inequalities by o(1). Because the quotient triangulation is finite and the squeeze inequalities are strict, there should be uniform slack on boundary-edge contraction and bend expansion. Apply Schwartz's T-pattern theorem to the regularized family, run the trapezoid inequalities with o(1) errors, and let epsilon go to zero. For beta<sqrt(3), the positive gap from sqrt(3)-beta should absorb the error.
key_ideas: Separate the topological T-pattern problem from the metric sqrt(3) calculation.
Use strict squeeze inequalities and compactness of the quotient triangulation to preserve inequalities under tiny perturbations.
A weak or limiting T-pattern is enough for an infimum/lower-bound proof; exact equality at beta=sqrt(3) need not be realized.
relevant_techniques: The Optimal Paper Moebius Band, https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.12641, Proposition 2.2: smoothness is used only to obtain a continuous bend foliation; the later proof needs the foliation and injectivity.
The Optimal Twisted Paper Cylinder, https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.14033, Lemma 2.1: flat or singular regions are filled by continuous families of straight bends, a useful model for fan resolution.

Closed Unstretchable Knotless Ribbons and the Wunderlich Functional, https://link.springer.com/content/pdf/10.1007/s00332-020-09630-z.pdf, Sect. 2.2 and Sect. 2.3: boundary ruling fans and flat-piece ruling choices clarify what can go wrong at boundary vertices.
directions.potential_obstacles: A naive perturbation may destroy the property that leaves map to straight segments under f, because only segments lying inside one affine triangle have straight images. The regularization must either perturb the triangulation and map together, or prove a limiting T-pattern theorem directly for the original fan family.

directions.name: Upper bound by anisotropic scaling of a triangular/Sadowsky band
directions.description: For any beta>sqrt(3), choose constants a<1<c with a beta / c > sqrt(3). Take an embedded PL or smoothly approximable paper Möbius band of width c and length a beta, i.e. aspect ratio a beta/c > sqrt(3), based on the Sadowsky/triangular construction. Pull it back to Sigma by A(x,y)=(a x, c y), which respects the glide reflection. Boundary edges are then shortened by factor a. A cross edge with target horizontal span Delta X has image length sqrt(Delta X^2+c^2), while its preimage in Sigma has length sqrt((Delta X/a)^2+1); by refining the clean triangulation so all Delta X are small, arrange sqrt(Delta X^2+c^2)>sqrt((Delta X/a)^2+1). This gives a realized beta.
key_ideas: Use extra aspect-ratio room beta>sqrt(3) to shrink horizontally and thicken vertically.
Refinement controls cross-edge slopes, making vertical thickening dominate horizontal shrinkage.
The limiting triangular Möbius band supplies the sharp model; the strict beta>sqrt(3) margin supplies the slack needed for embedding and strict squeeze.
relevant_techniques: Translation of Michael Sadowsky’s paper, https://arxiv.org/abs/1408.3034, width estimate and limiting sqrt(3) aspect ratio: concrete existence mechanism approaching the threshold from above.

Inverting a Cylinder Through Isometric Immersions and Isometric Embeddings, https://www.ams.org/journals/tran/1977-230-00/S0002-9947-1977-0474388-1/S0002-9947-1977-0474388-1.pdf, Section 15: Sadowsky-type upper construction for embedded Möbius strips for l>sqrt(3).
The Optimal Paper Moebius Band, https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.12641, Theorem 1.2: identifies the triangular Möbius band as the limiting model.

The Optimal Twisted Paper Cylinder, https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.14033, pseudofold smoothing technique: useful if one starts from a folded polygonal model and needs a smooth or separated embedded version.
directions.potential_obstacles: The literature guarantees smooth embedded paper bands and describes polygonal limiting folds, but the problem requires a clean triangulation with affine maps on triangles. The construction should therefore be made explicitly PL, or one must prove that the smooth/pseudofold model can be approximated by a clean affine triangulation while preserving strict squeeze inequalities.

directions.name: Finite tensegrity fallback
directions.description: If the T-pattern existence step in the PL setting remains stubborn, encode the problem as finite distance inequalities. Select finitely many bends or boundary vertices after quotienting. Each clean triangle gives inequalities |ridge image|<|ridge| and |side image|>|side|, and the quotient embedding gives over-under constraints for projected crossings. Then imitate Schwartz's finite tensegrity approach: define capacities for quadrilaterals or cyclic chains of selected bends and prove that any configuration satisfying the required incidence/crossing alternatives has capacity at least the sqrt(3) threshold.
key_ideas: Replace smooth or continuous foliation arguments by explicit inequalities on finitely many selected vertices and chords.
Use embeddedness through projected crossing diagrams and over-under consistency.
The squeeze inequalities are already edge-tensegrity inequalities, so this formulation is natural for the stated problem.
relevant_techniques: The Optimal Paper Moebius Band and Finite Tensegrity Calculations, https://www.math.brown.edu/~res/Papers/moebius.pdf, Equation (10): capacity lower bounds for compound tensegrities.

The Optimal Paper Moebius Band and Finite Tensegrity Calculations, https://www.math.brown.edu/~res/Papers/moebius.pdf, Lemma 2.2 Topology Lemma and Lemma 2.3 Geometry Lemma: crossing/incidence alternatives plus metric lower bounds.

The Optimal Paper Moebius Band and Finite Tensegrity Calculations, https://www.math.brown.edu/~res/Papers/moebius.pdf, Section 3.3 ridge curve construction and Lemma 3.2 angle range bounds: templates for controlling bend directions from global length.
directions.potential_obstacles: This is likely heavier than necessary. The finite-tensegrity paper itself is a research-note style reduction with difficult certified inequalities, so this route risks replacing one topological degeneracy issue by many algebraic verification problems.

directions.name: Near-critical stability as a diagnostic
directions.description: After a lower-bound proof is in place, mine its equality cases. For a sequence of realizations beta_n -> sqrt(3), the T-pattern parameter should satisfy t_n -> 1/sqrt(3), the T-pattern triangle should become equilateral, boundary contraction and bend expansion slack should vanish, and the images should converge toward the triangular Möbius band. This is not the shortest route to the infimum, but it is a powerful check on proposed constructions and on any attempted counterexample below sqrt(3).
key_ideas: Near equality forces every inequality in the T-pattern proof to be nearly sharp.
The limiting object should be the same equilateral triangular Möbius band as in the smooth theorem.
Stability can reveal hidden degeneracies in the PL bend family or upper construction.
relevant_techniques: The Optimal Paper Moebius Band, https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.12641, Theorem 1.2 and triangular-limit estimates: qualitative convergence to the triangular Möbius band.
On Nearly Optimal Paper Moebius Bands, https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.00572, Lemmas 3.3-3.9: quantitative control of t, triangle height, near-isosceles shape, and boundary closeness.
On Nearly Optimal Paper Moebius Bands, https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.00572, Lemmas 2.3-2.5: length slack converts to sqrt(epsilon)-scale geometric deviation.
directions.potential_obstacles: The clean-triangulation combinatorics may vary along a minimizing sequence, and squeeze margins may tend to zero. Stability should therefore be treated as a supporting tool, not as the first proof of the lower bound.

key_obstacles: The hard part is not the sqrt(3) arithmetic; it is producing a usable nondegenerate T-pattern from the PL clean-triangulation bend family. The second serious issue is the upper construction in the exact category demanded here: affine on clean triangles, strict boundary contraction, strict transverse expansion, and injectivity modulo G_beta. The smooth Sadowsky/Wunderlich variational literature is useful background but is probably not the right primary tool.
recommended_starting_point: Start with the Direct PL T-pattern lower bound. The very first lemma to prove is the squeeze-cevian lemma: in one clean triangle, shortening the ridge and lengthening the two sides forces every apex-to-ridge segment to be lengthened by concavity of squared length difference. Next prove that the quotient bend space is a circle and build the orientation-reversing supporting-line map. If the Borsuk-Ulam zero is degenerate because of an apex fan, switch immediately to the fan-regularization project; once a nondegenerate T-pattern exists, the cut-open trapezoid proof should be essentially the Schwartz Lemma G calculation with inequalities in the favorable direction.
